text: Hello everyone, this is Dr. Lovett. I thought I'd put together a quick five-minute video of the robotic cholecystectomy. I get a lot of questions from patients asking exactly what it is that we're doing and how the anatomy works, so I thought there's no better way than a video.
The surgeon places four robotic ports, each approximately eight millimeters in diameter, spanning from the left upper to the right lower part of the abdominal cavity.

The robotic surgical platform is used to manipulate graspers and a cautery hook to lyse small adhesions on the gallbladder, highlighted in green, and the surrounding anatomy. The liver and duodenum are visible, with the common bile duct running between the liver and small intestine. The surgeon begins dissection by opening the peritoneum overlying the gallbladder, using the hook to lift the tissue away, facilitated by the robotic system's wrist-like movement.

The surgeon performs a robotic cholecystectomy, carefully dissecting the gallbladder to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic duct and cystic artery are clearly visible entering the gallbladder, with no intervening structures between them and the liver. The common bile duct is also easily identifiable in this patient.

The surgeon places plastic clips across the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring not to occlude the common bile duct. The cystic artery is carefully cut using a cautery device, followed by the cystic duct. The gallbladder is then removed from the liver bed at an accelerated speed.

The surgeon observes a less distinct plane between the gallbladder and liver due to inflammation from previous gallbladder attacks. After the gallbladder is completely removed, the surgical team checks for bleeding around the clips. An assistant inserts a retrieval bag through the left upper port, and the gallbladder is placed inside for removal.
I appreciate you taking the time to watch. If you have any questions, please feel free to leave them in the comments.
